text:
The final chamber. An elderly lich sits at a desk buried in parchment. 'Adventurer,' it rasps, 'do you know ANYTHING about estate taxes? I've been dead for 300 years and they keep sending letters.'

The official lesson of "Dungeon Lich Taxes" is complicated; the useful lesson is that an investor has purchased the problem, removed everything useful, and listed the remainder as growth.

success.text: You have paid tolls, tithes, tariffs, and one fee for 'contempt of rock.' You know this enemy. You find the phylactery depreciation, the haunting-related home office deduction, AND a refund from a kingdom that still exists. The lich calls you 'the chosen one' with zero irony.
Three hours later, you've found a deduction for 'phylactery depreciation.' The lich weeps dust and pays you from its hoard.

fail.text: You make it worse. The lich now owes back taxes in two kingdoms, one of which no longer exists. It curses you out of pure administrative grief.

success.text: Mid-parry, the lich sees the artifact you carry and stops cold. 'You already emptied SOMEONE'S final chamber. Fine. I know how this ends.' It signs its hoard over to you like a resignation letter and sits back down with the parchment, muttering about succession planning.
The lich crumbles, almost relieved. Its final words: 'tell them... I'm... exempt.' The hoard is yours, minus filing fees.

fail.text: The lich raises the parchment sheaf, looks at you swaying on your feet, and lowers it. 'I am not filing paperwork on THAT. Sit. There is tea. It is dust, but it is tea.' You leave un-slain and faintly warmed.
The lich hits you with a sheaf of parchment. It's the deadliest thing in the room and it knows it.

choices.text: Suggest it just stop opening the letters

outcomes.text: The lich freezes mid-shuffle. 'You carry a third-circle curse AND you dispense wisdom? Professional courtesy demands an exchange.' It plucks the curse off you like lint and files it under MISC.
The lich stares at you. Three centuries of dread dissolve. 'I can... simply not?' It hands you gold with trembling claws. You've slain the real monster: obligation.

text:
The final door opens onto a vault. Actual treasure, actually glittering. A plaque reads: 'CONGRATULATIONS. PLEASE REVIEW YOUR EXPERIENCE ON THE WAY OUT.'

Behind the business of "Dungeon Finale Vault" sits an older truth: a merchant has found a way to own the risk while renting it back to everyone in danger.

outcomes.text: The vault's wards taste the artifact you carry and snap to attention, mistaking you for management. Inner cases you hadn't even noticed unlock themselves with an obsequious click. You are looted AT, efficiently.
Coins, trinkets, a thing that might be jewelry or dentistry. A clean haul.
The floor was a scale. The exit fee is calculated by weight. Still profitable, barely.

choices.text: Take only what fits in one hand

success.text: Restraint! The vault's guardian, a tired spectral accountant, waves you through and rounds your haul UP for politeness.

fail.text: Your one hand cramps around the single heaviest object. You leave with it and a limp.

outcomes.text: You sign the guest ledger with the name from the ballads. The quill trembles. A review from YOU could make or break a dungeon's whole season, and the stipend that materializes reflects this understanding precisely.
You write 'atmospheric, good bones, gap unmarked' on the guest ledger. A small stipend of appreciation materializes. The dungeon has pride.

choices.text: Rig a rope hoist and take a whole chest

outcomes.text: Pulleys, angles, one anchor point that is probably a gargoyle's elbow. The chest comes out whole and swinging. The rope does not survive the descent, but it dies doing what ropes love: holding entirely too much weight.

text:
The last chamber holds no monster. It holds a janitor, mopping around the bones. 'Mind the wet bones,' he says. 'You here for the hoard? It's behind the mop closet. I don't make the rules but I do clean up after them.'

A plaque may eventually misdescribe "Dungeon Finale Janitor". For now, the useful truth is that a supervisor has arrived to explain the task to everyone who has performed it for twenty years.

choices.text: Ask him to open the closet

success.text: He shrugs and unlocks it. 'Take the shiny ones. The cursed ones hum. You'll figure it out.' Solid professional advice.

fail.text: 'Closet's for staff.' Then your companion picks up the spare mop and starts on the far corner, unprompted. The janitor watches, visibly moved. 'Staff,' he declares, 'and family.' The closet opens.
'Closet's for staff.' He mops you gently but firmly toward the exit. You grab a souvenir from the lost-and-found on the way out.

choices.text: Fight him. He's clearly the boss

outcomes.text: You raise your fists. He looks at you, swaying, held together mostly by stubbornness, and sets the mop down. 'I don't fight furniture.' He sits you on a crate and produces soup from somewhere janitorial. You don't ask. You drink.
He deflects your entire offense with the mop. Decades of technique. You wake up outside, neatly propped against a rock, with a pity coin in your palm.
You were right. He IS the boss. But so many years of mopping has left him mostly retired, and he yields the closet with grace and one devastating shoulder throw.

You mop, and your glow lights corners no torch has reached in decades. He finds three lost rings, a set of teeth, and closure on a stain he'd been wondering about since spring. He splits the ring money and calls you the best lamp he's ever worked with. You choose to be flattered.
You mop. He talks. Three hours of dungeon gossip and a fair split of whatever rolls out from under the bones. Honest work.

text:
The final chamber is... a gift shop. Shelves of branded merchandise: tiny plush mimics, 'I SURVIVED {site}' mugs, novelty curse scrolls. A skeleton in a lanyard waves. 'Exit's through here. Everyone exits through here.'

Power calls "Dungeon Finale Gift Shop" an exception. The rule it conceals is simpler: a debt collector has arrived to repossess something the debtor grew personally.

outcomes.text: As you pay, the novelty curse scrolls all rustle toward your genuine curse like fans at a stage door. The skeleton is thrilled. 'A REAL one. We don't get many collectors.' It upgrades your purchase to the premium shelf, on the house.
The plush mimic is soft, judgmental, and possibly load-bearing to your happiness now.
The mug is real. The curse scroll was also, it turns out, real. Complimentary with purchase.

choices.text: Ask what happened to the actual boss

outcomes.text: You've seen enough dungeons by now to talk shop, so you do: foot traffic, adventurer seasonality, mimic overhead. The skeleton gets misty-socketed. 'Nobody ASKS.' It comps you a keychain, a mug, and a franchise brochure you will never, ever read.
'Franchised,' says the skeleton, adjusting its lanyard. 'The margins on terror are awful. Retail is forever.' It comps you a keychain for asking.

success.text: Amateurs palm things off shelves. You unlock the display case, the one with the GOOD snow globes, and relock it behind you on the way out. The skeleton never looks up. Craftsmanship respects craftsmanship.
You palm a snow globe of the dungeon itself. Inside it, a tiny you palms a tinier snow globe. Best not to think about it.

fail.text: The plush mimics were real mimics. Of course they were. Store security is impeccable and has teeth.

choices.text: License your name for the merch line

outcomes.text: The skeleton produces a contract with suspicious speed. By closing time there are mugs with your face on them, and the face is almost right. The royalty advance is real gold, and only slightly haunted by the knowledge of what you've become.
